Learning theory • interaction design • curriculum architecture

I design learning experiences that think with the student.

My work begins where many learners begin to lose confidence: complexity, overload, and uncertainty. I take that complexity apart, reveal its structure, and rebuild it into a clear, interactive journey.

Across healthcare, workforce education, medical billing and coding, mathematics, programming, and self-help tools, I design experiences that move learners from confusion to recognition, from recognition to reasoning, and from reasoning to confident action.

See selected work hilaryhomaii@gmail.com

Scenario-basedlearning experiences that make thinking visible
Hilary HomaiiInstructional design, curriculum development, and interactive learning design
Email me

A learning journey should feel like a guided investigation.

The clues are often scattered: the learner’s misconceptions, the structure of the task, the hidden rule, and the signal buried in the case. My role is to connect those pieces so the learner can see the pattern.

case studyThe learner enters through a real situation.
evidence extractionImportant details are separated from noise.
instructional insightThe path from clue to conclusion makes reasoning visible.
analysisPatterns and relationships begin to emerge.
pathwaysAlternative routes reveal what helps and what misleads.
decision practiceImportant cues are marked, emphasized, and used to support the right choice.
feedbackDirection becomes clearer through response and revision.
revisionThe next attempt becomes sharper and more confident.
What I pay attention toWhere learners hesitate, which evidence they overlook, which option attracts them, what they confuse, and what changes after feedback.
What the learner gainsA clearer mental model of how the problem works, how the parts connect, and how to approach the next challenge with more control.

How the pieces come together.

A learning experience may begin with many separate parts: content, objectives, learner needs, assessments, technology, feedback, and constraints. I organize those parts into one clear path.

content
practice
feedback
assessment
coherent learning experience
clear, structured, active, and measurable
reflection
transfer

I begin by understanding the people, the context, and the performance that matters. Then I map the sequence, choose the right form of practice, build feedback into the experience, and decide what evidence will show whether the design is working.

Select an icon to see the thinking and work behind each stage.

Behind the experience

Discovery and needs analysis

I speak with instructors, subject-matter experts, stakeholders, and learners to understand the audience, the performance gap, the environment, and the constraints. I separate a content request from the underlying learning or performance problem.

Bloom’s taxonomy should rise through the experience.

Bloom’s taxonomy shapes the pathway. The learner begins with understanding and steadily climbs into application, analysis, evaluation, and creation.

The climb matters.

Each stage prepares the learner for the next. Clear recognition supports understanding, understanding supports application, and application opens the door to analysis, evaluation, and creation. My design work often focuses on building that ascent with intention.

Rememberrecognize the term, the idea, the basic feature
Understandexplain the concept in clear language
Applyuse the idea in a realistic task or question
Analyzeseparate clues, patterns, and relationships
Evaluatejustify a decision and defend why it is best
Createproduce something new: explanation, pathway, plan, or solution

The work is iterative: we listen, plan, test, and refine.

This is the design intelligence behind the page. We talk, investigate, build, assess, and come back again. The cycle is part of the quality.

Every design should have a return path.

I look for signals: where the learner struggled, where the wording failed, where the visual hierarchy distracted, where the question exposed a misconception, where the interaction created insight, and what should happen next.

listen to the problem
define the outcome
design the sequence
prototype the interaction
evaluate learner evidence
refine and build again
smarter learning design
clearer decisions, stronger practice, better feedback
listen
diagnose
design
assess
refine
